title: Models — Thread and ThreadMember
description: Create AggregatorApp/Models/Thread.swift: `struct Thread: Decodable, Identifiable` mapping snake_case fields via CodingKeys — id, representativeTitle, rollingSummary?, knownFacts: [String], status, noveltyLabel?, firstSeen, lastUpdated, sourceCount, memberCount, imageURL?, hasUpdates, dismissed, topGrade?. Decode knownFacts defensively: use decodeIfPresent with a try? fallback so a malformed or absent field degrades to [] rather than throwing. Create AggregatorApp/Models/ThreadMember.swift: `struct ThreadMember: Decodable, Identifiable` mapping id, threadId, articleId, cleanTitle?, url?, sourceName?, publishedAt?, classificationLabel?, suppressed.
acceptance_criteria: Thread.knownFacts defaults to [] when the field is absent or malformed rather than throwing
Thread.imageURL is nil when image_url is absent in the JSON
Thread.topGrade is nil when top_grade is absent or null
Both types conform to Identifiable using their id field
Both types compile alongside PaginatedResponse without errors

handoff_summary.completed: Thread.swift and ThreadMember.swift created. xcodegen run, Xcode project regenerated, build passes with no errors.
handoff_summary.risks: sourceCount, memberCount, hasUpdates, and dismissed on Thread use decode (throwing) rather than decodeIfPresent. The OpenAPI spec marks them as not required but gives them defaults — FastAPI always serializes them, so this is expected to be safe. If the server ever omits them the decoder will throw.
handoff_summary.next_action: Tester agent should verify all acceptance criteria via unit tests: knownFacts absent/null/malformed → [], imageURL nil when absent, topGrade nil when absent/null, dismissed and suppressed Bool decoding, Identifiable conformance, and PaginatedResponse<Thread>/<ThreadMember> compile-time compatibility.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: No conflicts expected. These are new files. Downstream beads that use Thread or ThreadMember should confirm the field set (imageURL is String? not URL?, dates are String not Date).
handoff_summary.design_decisions: 1. knownFacts defensive decode: used `(try? container.decode([String].self, forKey: .knownFacts)) ?? []` rather than the more complex `decodeIfPresent + flatMap` pattern. `decode` throws on absent key, null value, and non-[String] items — all three cases collapse to [] via try? + nil-coalescing. This is simpler and handles all specified failure modes. 2. imageURL typed as String? not URL?: avoids a silent nil if the server returns a syntactically valid but non-RFC-3986 URL string. Callers that need a URL can construct one at use-site. 3. Dates typed as String: no date parsing in the model layer — avoids ISO8601 format assumptions and keeps the model portable. 4. ThreadMember uses synthesized Decodable (no custom init): all its optional fields (String?, Bool non-optional) map cleanly to decodeIfPresent/decode in the synthesized initializer. No defensive logic needed beyond what Swift provides for Optional properties. 5. Extra API fields (tier, relevance_score, new_facts, etc.) intentionally omitted — only the fields named in the bead description are included. The JSONDecoder silently ignores unknown keys.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Tester should cover: (a) Thread.knownFacts with absent key, null value, array-of-non-strings (e.g. [{...}]), and valid [String]; (b) Thread.imageURL with null and absent key both → nil; (c) Thread.topGrade with null and absent → nil; (d) Thread.dismissed = false and true; (e) ThreadMember.suppressed = false and true; (f) PaginatedResponse<Thread> and PaginatedResponse<ThreadMember> decode from a representative JSON payload; (g) Identifiable: Thread.id and ThreadMember.id are the synthesized id property satisfying the protocol.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: Fields not surfaced from the full OpenAPI ThreadResponse (tier, tier_reason, relevance_score, novelty_score, importance_score, diversity_score, time_sensitivity_score, source_diversity, confidence, deltas, source_list, surfaced) are intentionally absent — add them in a follow-up bead if UI features require them. ThreadMember similarly omits new_facts, reason, confidence, assigned_at. Date fields (firstSeen, lastUpdated, publishedAt) stored as String — callers needing formatted dates must parse at use-site.

conflict_risks: No conflicts expected. These are new files. Downstream beads that use Thread or ThreadMember should confirm the field set (imageURL is String? not URL?, dates are String not Date).

execution_history.summary: Created Thread.swift and ThreadMember.swift in AggregatorApp/Models/. Thread uses a custom init(from:) for defensive knownFacts decoding (try? … ?? []). ThreadMember uses synthesized Decodable with a CodingKeys enum. Both files compile cleanly after xcodegen regeneration.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

## Corrective bead scope

If your `bead_id` ends in `-corrective` or `-corrective-<N>` (e.g. `B-abc12345-corrective`, `B-abc12345-test-corrective-2`), you are running as a CORRECTIVE bead. Your scope is **strictly limited to fixing the specific failure that blocked the parent bead**. Do not:

- Add unrelated improvements, refactors, or cleanup, even if you notice them
- Reapply previously-reverted changes (the operator reverted them for a reason)
- Expand the surface area beyond what is needed to unblock the parent
- Touch files outside what the parent bead's `expected_files` / `expected_globs` covered, unless the failure is genuinely in another file and there is no smaller fix

If you notice an unrelated issue while fixing the corrective scope: **file a separate bead** via `takt bead create` describing what you saw, but do not fix it in this corrective. The operator will prioritize and dispatch.

When in doubt about scope: prefer the smallest possible fix. The reviewer of this corrective will check that the scope was respected.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `Swift` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: ``.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

## Memory

**Read memory at bead start.** Before touching any code, run three searches using `$TAKT_CMD` (injected by the orchestrator):

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace global
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>
$TAKT_CMD memory search "<bead topic keywords>" --namespace specs
```

Treat results as ambient context — apply relevant entries; skip entries that don't apply.

**Write to memory when you discover reusable project knowledge** — something that would have changed your approach if you had known it upfront, and is not already in CLAUDE.md or the guardrails.

```bash
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<concise fact>" --namespace global               # project-wide knowledge
$TAKT_CMD memory add "<discovery>" --namespace feature:<feature_root_id>  # feature-scoped
```

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
